title: Arrhythmia — Ventricular Fibrillation
oneLiner: A chaotic, pulseless ventricular rhythm causing cardiac arrest; the most common initial rhythm in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, reversible only by early defibrillation.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic vignette:**
  A 62-year-old man with a history of hypertension and chest pain that started 45 minutes ago suddenly collapses at the coffee station. He is unresponsive, apnoeic, and cyanosed. A nurse calls the arrest team. The cardiac monitor shows a coarse, irregular, chaotic waveform with no distinct QRS complexes. Palpation of the carotid artery reveals no pulse. Chest compressions are started immediately.

- **Dissect the vignette — pattern clues and their meaning:**
  - *Sudden collapse with loss of consciousness* → Loss of cardiac output; brain perfusion ceases within seconds. Tells you the rhythm is likely shockable (VF or pulseless VT).
  - *No pulse* → Mechanical contraction is absent; confirmation of cardiac arrest.
  - *Coarse, irregular, chaotic waveform on monitor* → Ventricular fibrillation: multiple re-entrant wavelets firing at 300–500/min; no coordinated depolarisation.
  - *Cyanosed, apnoeic* → Advanced life support needed; immediate defibrillation and CPR.
  - *Recent chest pain* → Suggests acute myocardial ischaemia as the trigger (the most common cause).

- **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
  - *Fine VF on the monitor* — appears nearly flat (like asystole). If you see a very low-amplitude, undulating baseline (often <0.1mV), **do not call it asystole**. Fine VF is still shockable; the rule is: if there is any organised electrical activity, it’s VF or VT until proven otherwise.
  - *VF in a patient with an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)* — the ICD may fire (you see pacing spikes or a shock artefact) but the rhythm recurs. This indicates failed first shock; need external defibrillation and magnet placement over ICD to disable anti-tachycardia pacing.
  - *No chest pain (quiet ischaemia)* — patients with diabetes, elderly, or post-surgical may not report chest pain; VF may be the first presentation. Look for hypotension, diaphoresis, or nausea before arrest.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- **Plain-language definition:**
  Ventricular fibrillation (VF) is a chaotic, disorganised electrical state of the ventricles in which hundreds of tiny re-entrant circuits fire simultaneously. No effective mechanical contraction occurs — the heart quivers like a bag of worms. The result is immediate loss of pulse, cardiac output, and consciousness.

- **Why this topic matters clinically — frequency, stakes, common pitfalls:**
  - VF is the **initial rhythm in approximately 40% of out-of-hospital cardiac arrests** and up to 30% of in-hospital arrests. Survival to discharge is only 10–20% overall, but **for every minute defibrillation is delayed, survival drops by 7–10%**.
  - Common pitfalls:
    - **Not shocking fine VF** — waiting for it to become coarse wastes time. Fine VF is shockable.
    - **Delaying defibrillation for intubation or IV access** — shocking takes priority (pulse check <10 seconds).
    - **Stopping chest compressions to analyse the rhythm** too long — minimise hands-off time.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
- **Story of development:**
  Normal ventricular depolarisation proceeds in a coordinated wave from Purkinje fibres to myocytes. In VF, regions of slowed conduction (e.g., due to ischaemia, fibrosis, electrolyte derangement) create blocks. Multiple wavelets of excitation wander around the ventricles, colliding and re-entering through circuits with varying refractory periods. The net effect: the electrical signal never completes a single organised contraction — it constantly circles, fractionates, and degenerates into chaos.

- **Why each key clinical sign happens:**
  - *No pulse* → No coordinated ventricular contraction → no stroke volume → absent pulse and blood pressure.
  - *Coarse waveform on ECG* → Large voltage differences between fibrillating myocytes; multiple wavelets produce a high-amplitude, irregular baseline (coarse VF = more electrical mass, better chance of defibrillation).
  - *Sustained unconsciousness* → Brain is perfused only by chest compressions (which deliver ~25% normal cardiac output).
  - *Chest pain before collapse* (when present) → Acute myocardial ischaemia alters membrane excitability → inactivation of sodium channels → slowed conduction → re-entrant substrate.

- **Connect pathophys to pattern recognition cues:**
  - The “coarse” vs “fine” distinction is purely amplitude: coarse VF has larger re-entrant loops with more viable myocardium; fine VF reflects fewer viable cells or longer duration (more energy has been depleted). This matters because **fine VF can respond to adrenaline (which increases contraction strength and VF amplitude)**, making it easier to defibrillate.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Causes | Stem clue |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| **Ischaemic (most common, ~80%)** | Acute coronary syndrome (MI), coronary spasm, previous infarction scar | Sudden collapse after chest pain; ST-elevation on pre-arrest ECG if available |
| **Electrolyte / metabolic** | Severe hypokalaemia (<3.0), hypomagnesaemia, hypocalcaemia, acidosis | In cardiac arrest: ECG shows prolonged QT prior to arrest; patient on diuretics or has diarrhoea |
| **Inherited channelopathies** | Long QT syndrome (LQTS), Brugada syndrome, catecholaminergic polymorphic VT | Young patient, syncope during exercise or emotional stress; family history of sudden death |
| **Drug toxicity** | Sodium-channel blockers (flecainide, propafenone — causes coved ST in Brugada pattern); tricyclic antidepressants; digoxin overdose | Overdose history; wide QRS on ECG before arrest; alkalinisation (NaHCO₃) helps if Na-channel blocker |
| **Structural heart disease** | Dilated cardiomyopathy, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC), cardiac sarcoidosis | Known LV dysfunction, previous arrhythmias, evidence of myocardial scar on imaging |
| **Acute triggers (non-cardiac)** | Severe hypoxia, hypothermia, trauma, electrocution, lightning strike | Context: drowning, electrocution, extreme hypothermia (core <30°C) |

**Most common cause:** **Acute myocardial ischaemia (STEMI/NSTEMI)**. Assume an ACS until proven otherwise — treat with primary PCI post-ROSC even if ECG is uninterpretable.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
- **Diagnostic criteria:** There is **no scoring system for VF** — the diagnosis is made by rhythm interpretation during cardiac arrest. **The single finding that confirms VF** is an irregular, chaotic, rapid waveform with no distinct P, QRS, or T waves, and no pulse.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|---------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **ECG (rhythm strip during arrest)** | VF waveform — coarse (>3mm amplitude) or fine (<3mm) | Confirm shockable rhythm | Coarse VF = better prognosis; fine VF may be mistaken for asystole — if any undulation, shock it |
| **Post-ROSQ 12-lead ECG** | ST-elevation, new LBBB, or deep T-wave inversion; may also show QT prolongation, Brugada sign | Identify ischaemic trigger; screen for channelopathy | ST-elevation → urgent coronary angiography; QT >500 ms → consider LQTS (give MgSO₄) |
| **Arterial blood gas (ABG)** | pH, lactate, K⁺, Ca²⁺, glucose | Identify acidosis, electrolytes, guide resuscitation | Lactate >5 mmol/L post-ROSC suggests poor prognosis; K⁺ <3.0 = replete aggressively; pH <7.2 = correct with ventilation first |
| **Serum electrolytes & Mg²⁺** | K⁺, Mg²⁺, corrected Ca²⁺ | Correct underlying metabolic triggers | K⁺ <3.0 → give KCl 40 mmol IV over 30 min; Mg²⁺ <2.0 → 2 g MgSO₄ IV bolus (also helps defibrillation threshold) |
| **Troponin (hs-cTn)** | Raised if myocardial infarction | Confirm ischaemic aetiology after ROSC | Troponin rise alone does not rule out other causes; may be elevated from prolonged resuscitation even without MI |
| **Toxicology screen** | Serum drug levels for TCA, flecainide, digoxin | Suspect overdose | Positive → specific antidotes (NaHCO₃ for TCA, Fab fragments for digoxin) |
| **Echocardiogram (immediately post-ROSC)** | Regional wall motion abnormality, LVEF, pericardial effusion | Assess structural cause and guide revascularisation | Segmental hypokinesis → likely ischaemic; global LV dysfunction → cardiomyopathy; effusion/tamponade — different diagnosis |

**Pattern-recognition finding that rules out VF:** **Organised rhythm (P, QRS, T) with pulse** — that’s not VF. **Flat line** with no wandering baseline in two different leads = asystole (not shockable). But remember: fine VF can look flat — always maximise gain and check two leads.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
#### Immediate (first hour): “The Golden 5 Minutes”
- **Defibrillate as soon as a manual defibrillator is available.**
  - *Algorithm:* Biphasic 200 J → 200 J → 200 J (monophasic: 360 J → 360 J → 360 J). Stacked shocks no longer recommended; single shock + immediate CPR for 2 min.
  - *Why:* VF is electricity-dependent; early shock converts to perfusing rhythm. Each minute without shock reduces survival by 10%.
- **High-quality CPR** at 100–120/min, depth 5–6 cm, full chest recoil. Minimise hands-off time (<10 seconds).
- **Airway:** Insert a supraglottic airway or endotracheal tube; give **oxygen** at 15 L/min once advanced airway is in place.
- **IV/IO access** — large-bore IV in antecubital fossa or IO in tibia/humerus.
- **Adrenaline (epinephrine) 1 mg IV/IO** every 3–5 minutes (first dose after second shock). *Why:* Increases coronary perfusion pressure during CPR, may convert fine VF to coarse VF.
- **Amiodarone** after 3 shocks: **300 mg IV/IO** bolus, then 150 mg after 5 shocks if still in VF. *Alternative:* Lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg if amiodarone unavailable.
- **Treat reversible causes** (4Hs & 4Ts):
  - Hypoxia: ensure oxygenation.
  - Hypo- / hyperkalaemia: correct K⁺, Mg²⁺.
  - Hypothermia: if core <30°C, limit shocks, rewarm with warm IV fluids + warm blankets.
  - Tension pneumothorax: needle decompression.
  - Tamponade: pericardiocentesis.
  - Thrombus (coronary): primary PCI post-ROSC.
  - Toxins: specific antidotes.

#### Ward (24–72 hours post-ROSC)
- **Targeted temperature management (TTM):** Cool to 32–36°C for 24 hours (target 34°C in many centres). *Why:* Reduces reperfusion injury and improves neurological outcome.
- **Coronary angiography + revascularisation** — if STEMI or high suspicion, proceed immediately. All patients with VF of uncertain cause should have angiography unless terminal or poor prognosis.
- **Optimise electrolytes:** Maintain K⁺ 4.0–4.5, Mg²⁺ >2.0, Ca²⁺ normal.
- **Antiarrhythmics:** Continue amiodarone for 24–48h if VF was recurrent (loading + infusion: 900 mg in 500 mL D5W over 24h). Consider beta-blocker (metoprolol 5 mg IV q6h, then PO) to reduce catecholamine drive.
- **Neuroprognostication:** GCS, pupillary light and corneal reflexes, EEG if prolonged coma. Avoid sedatives for assessment after 72h.

#### Long-term
- **Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD):** Indicated for survivors with ischaemic cardiomyopathy (LVEF ≤35%) or any non-reversible cause (channelopathy, idiopathic VF). **Do not forget to counsel about driving restrictions** (6–12 months depending on local rules).
- **Treatment of underlying cause:**
  - CAD: revascularisation, antiplatelets, statin.
  - Channelopathy: avoidance of QT-prolonging drugs, beta-blocker (for LQTS), flecainide for CPVT.
  - Drug-induced: avoid trigger.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**
- **Don’t shock asystole** — it does no good. But if you’re unsure between fine VF and asystole, shock once.
- **Don’t stop chest compressions for more than 10 seconds** during defibrillation or rhythm check.
- **Don’t give calcium** routinely — only if hyperkalaemia, hypocalcaemia, or calcium-channel blocker overdose.
- **Don’t use sodium bicarbonate** unless severe acidosis (pH <7.1) or tricyclic overdose — it may impair oxygen delivery.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
The key mimics of VF are rhythms causing pulseless cardiac arrest. The table below compares three primary mimics.

| Feature | Ventricular Fibrillation (VF) | Asystole | Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA) | Torsades de Pointes (polymorphic VT) |
|---------|-------------------------------|----------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Rhythm** | Chaotic, irregular, no QRS | Flat line (no electrical activity) | Any organized rhythm (sinus, AF, etc.) — no pulse | QRS complexes twisting around baseline, often with long QT |
| **Pulse** | Absent | Absent | Absent | Usually absent (but can be perfusing if brief) |
| **ECG waveform** | Coarse or fine undulations | Straight line (isoelectric) | Normal-looking complexes but no mechanical output | “Twisting” morphology; rate 150–300/min |
| **Treatment** | Shock (defibrillation) | No shock — give adrenaline, treat cause | No shock — treat reversible causes (hypovolaemia, tamponade, PE, etc.) | Shock (defibrillation) — same as VF, but give **MgSO₄ 2 g IV** |
| **Key differentiator** | Presence of any “wave” → shockable | Absolute flat line in >1 lead → not shockable | Organized rhythm + no pulse → PEA | Pre-arrest ECG shows **prolonged QT**; often precipitated by hypokalaemia, bradycardia |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Death (if untreated):** VF that is not defibrillated **within 10 minutes** has a near-zero chance of survival. Fine VF degenerates to asystole (irreversible).
- **Hypoxic brain injury (if delay to ROSC):** Cerebral hypoxia causes permanent damage. Signs: fixed dilated pupils, absent corneal reflex, decerebrate posturing. **What to do:** TTM, EEG, later neuroimaging (CT/MRI) to assess anoxic injury.
- **Myocardial stunning / cardiogenic shock:** Prolonged VF and CPR cause global LV dysfunction. Signs: hypotension, pulmonary oedema, low cardiac output despite ROSC. **What to do:** inotropes (dobutamine), intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) or ECMO if refractory.
- **Recurrent VF (electrical storm):** >3 episodes in 24h. Signs: multiple shocks delivered. **What to do:** deep sedation (propofol), correction of ischaemia, amiodarone + lidocaine, consider stellate ganglion block.
- **Late-presentation patterns (exam stems):**
  - “A 70-year-old found unresponsive on the floor, no resuscitation for 20 minutes.” → EEG shows burst-suppression; prognosis is poor. **Do not rush to ICD** — discuss with family.
  - “Patient had VF arrest, shocked, ROSC, but remains comatose with absent pupillary light reflexes at 72h.” → Likely severe anoxic injury; neuroprognostication: consider withdrawal of life support.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
- **Mnemonic for reversible causes:** **4Hs & 4Ts**
  - Hypoxia
  - Hypovolaemia
  - Hypo- / hyperkalaemia
  - Hypothermia
  - Tension pneumothorax
  - Tamponade (cardiac)
  - Toxins
  - Thrombosis (coronary or pulmonary)

- **Mnemonic for antiarrhythmic order in VF:** **“Shock & Adrenaline; then Amiodarone, then Lidocaine”** (S-A-A-L)
  - Shock (3 shocks)
  - Adrenaline 1 mg after 2nd shock
  - Amiodarone 300 mg after 3rd shock
  - Lidocaine (if amiodarone fails or unavailable)

- **5 High-Yield Pearls:**
  1. **“Don’t pace VF.”** Pacing is useless in VF (no organised electrical activity). Always shock.
  2. **“Fine VF + doubt = shock.”** If you cannot decide between fine VF and asystole, give one shock — it may convert and harms nothing.
  3. **“Adrenaline before 3rd shock if rhythm is fine.”** A small dose (1 mg) may increase VF amplitude, making defibrillation more likely.
  4. **“After ROSC, look for the trigger: ECG, troponin, echo.”** Do not send for CT chest unless PE is suspected.
  5. **“Don’t forget magnesium in Torsades de Pointes.”** For VF that occurs in a patient with prolonged QT, give 2 g MgSO₄ IV over 1–2 min, then shock.

- **Common ward / exam mistakes:**
  - **Calling fine VF “asystole” and not shocking.** Always maximise gain and check two leads — if any waveform, treat as VF.
  - **Stopping CPR to check rhythm immediately after shock.** Wait 2 minutes of CPR before rhythm check — except if signs of life return.
  - **Giving amiodarone before the third shock.** It is a rhythm-stabiliser, not first-line (adrenaline is more urgent).
  - **Forgetting to ask about drug history (TCA, flecainide).** If present, give **sodium bicarbonate 50–100 mmol IV** (1–2 ampoules) before defibrillation.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call the crash team / code blue:**
  - Any collapsed patient with absent pulse and unresponsiveness — **immediately activate the cardiac arrest team** (dial emergency number in your hospital). Do not wait for monitor.
- **When to call a senior/ICU post-ROSC:**
  - **Any ROSC** — call ICU for targeted temperature management and further management.
  - **VF associated with STEMI** — inform interventional cardiology for emergency PCI.
  - **Recurrent VF (electrical storm)** — call senior cardiologist, consider deep sedation, mechanical support (IABP/ECMO).
  - **Lactate >5 mmol/L** post-ROSC — indicates severe shock; escalate to ICU, consider vasopressors.
  - **GCS <8** after ROSC — consider therapeutic hypothermia; need airway protection (ICU).
  - **NEWS2** is not applicable during cardiac arrest itself, but after ROSC: a total NEWS2 >7 with any single parameter at 3 (respiratory rate ≤8 or ≥25, oxygen saturations ≤91%, systolic BP ≤90, pulse ≤40 or ≥130, temperature ≤35°C, new confusion) should trigger immediate senior review.
- **Specific thresholds for urgent escalation:**
  - **Systolic BP <90 mmHg** despite fluid bolus → start noradrenaline, call ICU.
  - **PaO₂ <60 mmHg** on 100% O₂ or SpO₂ <90% → escalate airway (intubate if not already), call anaesthesia.
  - **pH <7.2** on ABG despite optimised ventilation → consider bicarbonate, call ICU.
  - **Hypothermia (<32°C)** → rewarm with forced-air blanket + warm IV fluids, avoid active external warming of hands/feet (may cause arrhythmia).

rawMarkdown:
## Arrhythmia — Ventricular Fibrillation
**One-liner:** A chaotic, pulseless ventricular rhythm causing cardiac arrest; the most common initial rhythm in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, reversible only by early defibrillation.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
- **Classic vignette:**
  A 62-year-old man with a history of hypertension and chest pain that started 45 minutes ago suddenly collapses at the coffee station. He is unresponsive, apnoeic, and cyanosed. A nurse calls the arrest team. The cardiac monitor shows a coarse, irregular, chaotic waveform with no distinct QRS complexes. Palpation of the carotid artery reveals no pulse. Chest compressions are started immediately.

- **Dissect the vignette — pattern clues and their meaning:**
  - *Sudden collapse with loss of consciousness* → Loss of cardiac output; brain perfusion ceases within seconds. Tells you the rhythm is likely shockable (VF or pulseless VT).
  - *No pulse* → Mechanical contraction is absent; confirmation of cardiac arrest.
  - *Coarse, irregular, chaotic waveform on monitor* → Ventricular fibrillation: multiple re-entrant wavelets firing at 300–500/min; no coordinated depolarisation.
  - *Cyanosed, apnoeic* → Advanced life support needed; immediate defibrillation and CPR.
  - *Recent chest pain* → Suggests acute myocardial ischaemia as the trigger (the most common cause).

- **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
  - *Fine VF on the monitor* — appears nearly flat (like asystole). If you see a very low-amplitude, undulating baseline (often <0.1mV), **do not call it asystole**. Fine VF is still shockable; the rule is: if there is any organised electrical activity, it’s VF or VT until proven otherwise.
  - *VF in a patient with an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)* — the ICD may fire (you see pacing spikes or a shock artefact) but the rhythm recurs. This indicates failed first shock; need external defibrillation and magnet placement over ICD to disable anti-tachycardia pacing.
  - *No chest pain (quiet ischaemia)* — patients with diabetes, elderly, or post-surgical may not report chest pain; VF may be the first presentation. Look for hypotension, diaphoresis, or nausea before arrest.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
- **Plain-language definition:**
  Ventricular fibrillation (VF) is a chaotic, disorganised electrical state of the ventricles in which hundreds of tiny re-entrant circuits fire simultaneously. No effective mechanical contraction occurs — the heart quivers like a bag of worms. The result is immediate loss of pulse, cardiac output, and consciousness.

- **Why this topic matters clinically — frequency, stakes, common pitfalls:**
  - VF is the **initial rhythm in approximately 40% of out-of-hospital cardiac arrests** and up to 30% of in-hospital arrests. Survival to discharge is only 10–20% overall, but **for every minute defibrillation is delayed, survival drops by 7–10%**.
  - Common pitfalls:
    - **Not shocking fine VF** — waiting for it to become coarse wastes time. Fine VF is shockable.
    - **Delaying defibrillation for intubation or IV access** — shocking takes priority (pulse check <10 seconds).
    - **Stopping chest compressions to analyse the rhythm** too long — minimise hands-off time.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
- **Story of development:**
  Normal ventricular depolarisation proceeds in a coordinated wave from Purkinje fibres to myocytes. In VF, regions of slowed conduction (e.g., due to ischaemia, fibrosis, electrolyte derangement) create blocks. Multiple wavelets of excitation wander around the ventricles, colliding and re-entering through circuits with varying refractory periods. The net effect: the electrical signal never completes a single organised contraction — it constantly circles, fractionates, and degenerates into chaos.

- **Why each key clinical sign happens:**
  - *No pulse* → No coordinated ventricular contraction → no stroke volume → absent pulse and blood pressure.
  - *Coarse waveform on ECG* → Large voltage differences between fibrillating myocytes; multiple wavelets produce a high-amplitude, irregular baseline (coarse VF = more electrical mass, better chance of defibrillation).
  - *Sustained unconsciousness* → Brain is perfused only by chest compressions (which deliver ~25% normal cardiac output).
  - *Chest pain before collapse* (when present) → Acute myocardial ischaemia alters membrane excitability → inactivation of sodium channels → slowed conduction → re-entrant substrate.

- **Connect pathophys to pattern recognition cues:**
  - The “coarse” vs “fine” distinction is purely amplitude: coarse VF has larger re-entrant loops with more viable myocardium; fine VF reflects fewer viable cells or longer duration (more energy has been depleted). This matters because **fine VF can respond to adrenaline (which increases contraction strength and VF amplitude)**, making it easier to defibrillate.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Category | Causes | Stem clue |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| **Ischaemic (most common, ~80%)** | Acute coronary syndrome (MI), coronary spasm, previous infarction scar | Sudden collapse after chest pain; ST-elevation on pre-arrest ECG if available |
| **Electrolyte / metabolic** | Severe hypokalaemia (<3.0), hypomagnesaemia, hypocalcaemia, acidosis | In cardiac arrest: ECG shows prolonged QT prior to arrest; patient on diuretics or has diarrhoea |
| **Inherited channelopathies** | Long QT syndrome (LQTS), Brugada syndrome, catecholaminergic polymorphic VT | Young patient, syncope during exercise or emotional stress; family history of sudden death |
| **Drug toxicity** | Sodium-channel blockers (flecainide, propafenone — causes coved ST in Brugada pattern); tricyclic antidepressants; digoxin overdose | Overdose history; wide QRS on ECG before arrest; alkalinisation (NaHCO₃) helps if Na-channel blocker |
| **Structural heart disease** | Dilated cardiomyopathy, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC), cardiac sarcoidosis | Known LV dysfunction, previous arrhythmias, evidence of myocardial scar on imaging |
| **Acute triggers (non-cardiac)** | Severe hypoxia, hypothermia, trauma, electrocution, lightning strike | Context: drowning, electrocution, extreme hypothermia (core <30°C) |

**Most common cause:** **Acute myocardial ischaemia (STEMI/NSTEMI)**. Assume an ACS until proven otherwise — treat with primary PCI post-ROSC even if ECG is uninterpretable.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
- **Diagnostic criteria:** There is **no scoring system for VF** — the diagnosis is made by rhythm interpretation during cardiac arrest. **The single finding that confirms VF** is an irregular, chaotic, rapid waveform with no distinct P, QRS, or T waves, and no pulse.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|---------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **ECG (rhythm strip during arrest)** | VF waveform — coarse (>3mm amplitude) or fine (<3mm) | Confirm shockable rhythm | Coarse VF = better prognosis; fine VF may be mistaken for asystole — if any undulation, shock it |
| **Post-ROSQ 12-lead ECG** | ST-elevation, new LBBB, or deep T-wave inversion; may also show QT prolongation, Brugada sign | Identify ischaemic trigger; screen for channelopathy | ST-elevation → urgent coronary angiography; QT >500 ms → consider LQTS (give MgSO₄) |
| **Arterial blood gas (ABG)** | pH, lactate, K⁺, Ca²⁺, glucose | Identify acidosis, electrolytes, guide resuscitation | Lactate >5 mmol/L post-ROSC suggests poor prognosis; K⁺ <3.0 = replete aggressively; pH <7.2 = correct with ventilation first |
| **Serum electrolytes & Mg²⁺** | K⁺, Mg²⁺, corrected Ca²⁺ | Correct underlying metabolic triggers | K⁺ <3.0 → give KCl 40 mmol IV over 30 min; Mg²⁺ <2.0 → 2 g MgSO₄ IV bolus (also helps defibrillation threshold) |
| **Troponin (hs-cTn)** | Raised if myocardial infarction | Confirm ischaemic aetiology after ROSC | Troponin rise alone does not rule out other causes; may be elevated from prolonged resuscitation even without MI |
| **Toxicology screen** | Serum drug levels for TCA, flecainide, digoxin | Suspect overdose | Positive → specific antidotes (NaHCO₃ for TCA, Fab fragments for digoxin) |
| **Echocardiogram (immediately post-ROSC)** | Regional wall motion abnormality, LVEF, pericardial effusion | Assess structural cause and guide revascularisation | Segmental hypokinesis → likely ischaemic; global LV dysfunction → cardiomyopathy; effusion/tamponade — different diagnosis |

**Pattern-recognition finding that rules out VF:** **Organised rhythm (P, QRS, T) with pulse** — that’s not VF. **Flat line** with no wandering baseline in two different leads = asystole (not shockable). But remember: fine VF can look flat — always maximise gain and check two leads.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
#### Immediate (first hour): “The Golden 5 Minutes”
- **Defibrillate as soon as a manual defibrillator is available.**
  - *Algorithm:* Biphasic 200 J → 200 J → 200 J (monophasic: 360 J → 360 J → 360 J). Stacked shocks no longer recommended; single shock + immediate CPR for 2 min.
  - *Why:* VF is electricity-dependent; early shock converts to perfusing rhythm. Each minute without shock reduces survival by 10%.
- **High-quality CPR** at 100–120/min, depth 5–6 cm, full chest recoil. Minimise hands-off time (<10 seconds).
- **Airway:** Insert a supraglottic airway or endotracheal tube; give **oxygen** at 15 L/min once advanced airway is in place.
- **IV/IO access** — large-bore IV in antecubital fossa or IO in tibia/humerus.
- **Adrenaline (epinephrine) 1 mg IV/IO** every 3–5 minutes (first dose after second shock). *Why:* Increases coronary perfusion pressure during CPR, may convert fine VF to coarse VF.
- **Amiodarone** after 3 shocks: **300 mg IV/IO** bolus, then 150 mg after 5 shocks if still in VF. *Alternative:* Lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg if amiodarone unavailable.
- **Treat reversible causes** (4Hs & 4Ts):
  - Hypoxia: ensure oxygenation.
  - Hypo- / hyperkalaemia: correct K⁺, Mg²⁺.
  - Hypothermia: if core <30°C, limit shocks, rewarm with warm IV fluids + warm blankets.
  - Tension pneumothorax: needle decompression.
  - Tamponade: pericardiocentesis.
  - Thrombus (coronary): primary PCI post-ROSC.
  - Toxins: specific antidotes.

#### Ward (24–72 hours post-ROSC)
- **Targeted temperature management (TTM):** Cool to 32–36°C for 24 hours (target 34°C in many centres). *Why:* Reduces reperfusion injury and improves neurological outcome.
- **Coronary angiography + revascularisation** — if STEMI or high suspicion, proceed immediately. All patients with VF of uncertain cause should have angiography unless terminal or poor prognosis.
- **Optimise electrolytes:** Maintain K⁺ 4.0–4.5, Mg²⁺ >2.0, Ca²⁺ normal.
- **Antiarrhythmics:** Continue amiodarone for 24–48h if VF was recurrent (loading + infusion: 900 mg in 500 mL D5W over 24h). Consider beta-blocker (metoprolol 5 mg IV q6h, then PO) to reduce catecholamine drive.
- **Neuroprognostication:** GCS, pupillary light and corneal reflexes, EEG if prolonged coma. Avoid sedatives for assessment after 72h.

#### Long-term
- **Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD):** Indicated for survivors with ischaemic cardiomyopathy (LVEF ≤35%) or any non-reversible cause (channelopathy, idiopathic VF). **Do not forget to counsel about driving restrictions** (6–12 months depending on local rules).
- **Treatment of underlying cause:**
  - CAD: revascularisation, antiplatelets, statin.
  - Channelopathy: avoidance of QT-prolonging drugs, beta-blocker (for LQTS), flecainide for CPVT.
  - Drug-induced: avoid trigger.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**
- **Don’t shock asystole** — it does no good. But if you’re unsure between fine VF and asystole, shock once.
- **Don’t stop chest compressions for more than 10 seconds** during defibrillation or rhythm check.
- **Don’t give calcium** routinely — only if hyperkalaemia, hypocalcaemia, or calcium-channel blocker overdose.
- **Don’t use sodium bicarbonate** unless severe acidosis (pH <7.1) or tricyclic overdose — it may impair oxygen delivery.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
The key mimics of VF are rhythms causing pulseless cardiac arrest. The table below compares three primary mimics.

| Feature | Ventricular Fibrillation (VF) | Asystole | Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA) | Torsades de Pointes (polymorphic VT) |
|---------|-------------------------------|----------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Rhythm** | Chaotic, irregular, no QRS | Flat line (no electrical activity) | Any organized rhythm (sinus, AF, etc.) — no pulse | QRS complexes twisting around baseline, often with long QT |
| **Pulse** | Absent | Absent | Absent | Usually absent (but can be perfusing if brief) |
| **ECG waveform** | Coarse or fine undulations | Straight line (isoelectric) | Normal-looking complexes but no mechanical output | “Twisting” morphology; rate 150–300/min |
| **Treatment** | Shock (defibrillation) | No shock — give adrenaline, treat cause | No shock — treat reversible causes (hypovolaemia, tamponade, PE, etc.) | Shock (defibrillation) — same as VF, but give **MgSO₄ 2 g IV** |
| **Key differentiator** | Presence of any “wave” → shockable | Absolute flat line in >1 lead → not shockable | Organized rhythm + no pulse → PEA | Pre-arrest ECG shows **prolonged QT**; often precipitated by hypokalaemia, bradycardia |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
- **Death (if untreated):** VF that is not defibrillated **within 10 minutes** has a near-zero chance of survival. Fine VF degenerates to asystole (irreversible).
- **Hypoxic brain injury (if delay to ROSC):** Cerebral hypoxia causes permanent damage. Signs: fixed dilated pupils, absent corneal reflex, decerebrate posturing. **What to do:** TTM, EEG, later neuroimaging (CT/MRI) to assess anoxic injury.
- **Myocardial stunning / cardiogenic shock:** Prolonged VF and CPR cause global LV dysfunction. Signs: hypotension, pulmonary oedema, low cardiac output despite ROSC. **What to do:** inotropes (dobutamine), intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) or ECMO if refractory.
- **Recurrent VF (electrical storm):** >3 episodes in 24h. Signs: multiple shocks delivered. **What to do:** deep sedation (propofol), correction of ischaemia, amiodarone + lidocaine, consider stellate ganglion block.
- **Late-presentation patterns (exam stems):**
  - “A 70-year-old found unresponsive on the floor, no resuscitation for 20 minutes.” → EEG shows burst-suppression; prognosis is poor. **Do not rush to ICD** — discuss with family.
  - “Patient had VF arrest, shocked, ROSC, but remains comatose with absent pupillary light reflexes at 72h.” → Likely severe anoxic injury; neuroprognostication: consider withdrawal of life support.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
- **Mnemonic for reversible causes:** **4Hs & 4Ts**
  - Hypoxia
  - Hypovolaemia
  - Hypo- / hyperkalaemia
  - Hypothermia
  - Tension pneumothorax
  - Tamponade (cardiac)
  - Toxins
  - Thrombosis (coronary or pulmonary)

- **Mnemonic for antiarrhythmic order in VF:** **“Shock & Adrenaline; then Amiodarone, then Lidocaine”** (S-A-A-L)
  - Shock (3 shocks)
  - Adrenaline 1 mg after 2nd shock
  - Amiodarone 300 mg after 3rd shock
  - Lidocaine (if amiodarone fails or unavailable)

- **5 High-Yield Pearls:**
  1. **“Don’t pace VF.”** Pacing is useless in VF (no organised electrical activity). Always shock.
  2. **“Fine VF + doubt = shock.”** If you cannot decide between fine VF and asystole, give one shock — it may convert and harms nothing.
  3. **“Adrenaline before 3rd shock if rhythm is fine.”** A small dose (1 mg) may increase VF amplitude, making defibrillation more likely.
  4. **“After ROSC, look for the trigger: ECG, troponin, echo.”** Do not send for CT chest unless PE is suspected.
  5. **“Don’t forget magnesium in Torsades de Pointes.”** For VF that occurs in a patient with prolonged QT, give 2 g MgSO₄ IV over 1–2 min, then shock.

- **Common ward / exam mistakes:**
  - **Calling fine VF “asystole” and not shocking.** Always maximise gain and check two leads — if any waveform, treat as VF.
  - **Stopping CPR to check rhythm immediately after shock.** Wait 2 minutes of CPR before rhythm check — except if signs of life return.
  - **Giving amiodarone before the third shock.** It is a rhythm-stabiliser, not first-line (adrenaline is more urgent).
  - **Forgetting to ask about drug history (TCA, flecainide).** If present, give **sodium bicarbonate 50–100 mmol IV** (1–2 ampoules) before defibrillation.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
- **When to call the crash team / code blue:**
  - Any collapsed patient with absent pulse and unresponsiveness — **immediately activate the cardiac arrest team** (dial emergency number in your hospital). Do not wait for monitor.
- **When to call a senior/ICU post-ROSC:**
  - **Any ROSC** — call ICU for targeted temperature management and further management.
  - **VF associated with STEMI** — inform interventional cardiology for emergency PCI.
  - **Recurrent VF (electrical storm)** — call senior cardiologist, consider deep sedation, mechanical support (IABP/ECMO).
  - **Lactate >5 mmol/L** post-ROSC — indicates severe shock; escalate to ICU, consider vasopressors.
  - **GCS <8** after ROSC — consider therapeutic hypothermia; need airway protection (ICU).
  - **NEWS2** is not applicable during cardiac arrest itself, but after ROSC: a total NEWS2 >7 with any single parameter at 3 (respiratory rate ≤8 or ≥25, oxygen saturations ≤91%, systolic BP ≤90, pulse ≤40 or ≥130, temperature ≤35°C, new confusion) should trigger immediate senior review.
- **Specific thresholds for urgent escalation:**
  - **Systolic BP <90 mmHg** despite fluid bolus → start noradrenaline, call ICU.
  - **PaO₂ <60 mmHg** on 100% O₂ or SpO₂ <90% → escalate airway (intubate if not already), call anaesthesia.
  - **pH <7.2** on ABG despite optimised ventilation → consider bicarbonate, call ICU.
  - **Hypothermia (<32°C)** → rewarm with forced-air blanket + warm IV fluids, avoid active external warming of hands/feet (may cause arrhythmia).
